behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces a route no more than 1.5x the optimal length for up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The heuristic is a pure algorithm operating on coordinates — no module handoff. The failure mode (bad route quality) is entirely within the heuristic function itself. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess involved — just in-memory coordinate arithmetic. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. For small N (≤6 stops), brute-force optimal is computable in-process, allowing direct ratio comparison. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the route quality failure does not depend on how modules hand data to each other; it depends solely on the algorithm's internal logic.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a set of synthetic coordinate inputs (up to 6 stops where brute-force optimal is feasible, up to 20 for ratio-bound check with a known lower bound). Call the heuristic function directly. Assert that the returned route length ≤ 1.5 × the computed optimal (or lower-bound approximation). No mocks needed; input is pure coordinate data.

behaviors.description: Delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from consecutive-stop distances and configured average speed per road type.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The computation is distance / speed = time — a deterministic arithmetic function. The failure mode (wrong estimate) lives entirely inside the time-window calculation function, not at a module boundary. REAL-INFRA: No I/O. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the function takes distances and a speed config as input; no module coordination is required to expose a wrong result.
behaviors.test_description: Call the time-window estimation function directly with explicit distance values (e.g., 10 km) and per-road-type speed configs (e.g., 50 km/h urban, 100 km/h highway). Assert the resulting window equals distance / speed to the expected precision. Parametrize over multiple road types and edge cases (zero distance, boundary speeds).

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The validator takes a total duration and a remaining-shift value and returns a rejection with the excess. The entire failure mode — wrong rejection decision or wrong excess value — lives in a single comparison/subtraction. No module handoff is needed to expose a bug here. REAL-INFRA: No. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — the validator's contract (inputs: total_time, remaining_shift; output: excess or None) is fully testable in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Call the shift-hours validator function directly with (total_time, remaining_shift) pairs covering: total < remaining (expect pass), total == remaining (boundary — expect pass or rejection depending on spec), total > remaining (expect rejection with exact excess = total − remaining). Assert return values precisely. No infrastructure needed.

behaviors.description: When one address fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop, optimizes the remaining addresses, and reports which were skipped.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans multiple module boundaries: geocoding-result handler must propagate the skip signal, the stop-list builder must exclude the failed address, the optimizer must receive only valid coordinates, and the skip-reporting module must surface the skipped address. A unit test of any single function cannot catch a handoff bug where, e.g., the skipped address silently passes as a null coordinate to the optimizer. REAL-INFRA: A stub/fake geocoding HTTP client can reproduce the 'no results' response in-process — no real HTTP needed. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking at the service boundary would hide the coordination bugs. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected — the failure does not require a live geocoding endpoint; an in-process fake returning a 'no results' payload exercises the same code paths.
behaviors.test_description: Wire together the geocoding client (using a fake/stub that returns no-results for one specific address and valid coordinates for others), the stop-list builder, the TSP optimizer, and the skip reporter. Submit a list of 4 addresses where 1 is configured to fail. Assert: (a) the optimized route contains exactly 3 stops, (b) the failed address appears in the skipped-addresses report, (c) no null or garbage coordinate appears in the optimizer input.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The consolidation logic — detecting equal addresses and merging their delivery-item lists — is the core algorithmic concern. The failure mode (duplicate not detected, items not merged) lives inside the deduplication/consolidation function itself. REAL-INFRA: No I/O. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered for the 'not visited twice' claim, but this property is a consequence of correct deduplication: if the output stop list contains no duplicates, the optimizer cannot visit the address twice. The failure is in the consolidation step, not in the handoff to the optimizer.
behaviors.test_description: Call the deduplication/consolidation function directly with an input list containing two entries for the same address, each carrying distinct delivery items. Assert: (a) the output contains exactly one stop for that address, (b) the stop's delivery-item list is the union/concatenation of both inputs. Also test that distinct addresses are not merged. No mocks or infrastructure needed.
